file_name: Golnoori et al. - 2023 - Metaheuristic algorithm based hyper-parameters optimization for skin lesion classification.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning (Convolutional Neural Networks) and metaheuristic optimization algorithms (Genetic Algorithm, PSO, DE) to optimize hyper-parameters and architecture for skin lesion classification on the ISIC 2017 and ISIC 2018 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a decision support system for skin lesion classification. It employs metaheuristic optimization algorithms (Genetic Algorithm, PSO, DE) to optimize hyper-parameters and architecture of deep neural networks (CNNs) on the ISIC 2017 and ISIC 2018 datasets, reporting specific accuracy and F1-score results (e.g., 81.6% accuracy on ISIC 2017). The structure includes an abstract, introduction, related studies, methodology, experimental results, and conclusion, which is characteristic of a primary research article.

title: Metaheuristic algorithm based hyper-parameters optimization for skin lesion classification
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of skin lesion classification by optimizing the architecture and hyper-parameters of deep neural networks using metaheuristic algorithms, specifically Genetic Algorithm, Particle Swarm Optimization, and Differential Evolution. The authors evaluated these optimizations on both pre-trained models (such as DenseNet-201, ResNet-50, Inception-v3, VGG16, and Xception) and networks trained from scratch using the ISIC 2017 and ISIC 2018 datasets. Deep features extracted from the optimized models were fused in pairs and classified using a K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) classifier. The method achieved an accuracy of 81.6% and an F1-score of 80.9% on the ISIC 2017 dataset, and an accuracy of 90.1% with an F1-score of 89.8% on the ISIC 2018 dataset. The results demonstrate that the proposed approach outperforms similar methods in classifying seven and three classes of images without requiring heavy preprocessing or segmentation steps.

lesion_types_studied.details: Studied in ISIC 2018 dataset (2285 images) and ISIC 2017 dataset. Identified as the most dangerous type of skin cancer in the abstract and introduction.
Mentioned in the introduction as having high resemblance to melanoma, making diagnosis difficult.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion classes (e.g., Melanoma, Nevus, Benign Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Vascular Lesion, Dermatofibroma, Actinic Keratosis)
all_task_types.details: The paper focuses on skin lesion classification using deep neural networks and KNN classifiers on the ISIC 2017 and ISIC 2018 datasets. The task involves categorizing images into discrete diagnostic categories (seven classes for ISIC 2018 and three classes for ISIC 2017). Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, which are standard metrics for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2017 challenge, containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions.

use.training: Used for training the deep neural networks and KNN classifier.
use.validation: Used for validation during the hyper-parameter optimization process.
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of the classification performance.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2018 challenge, containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions with seven classes. The official test and validation sets lacked ground-truth labels at the time of the study.

use.training: The training set (10,015 images) was used for model training and evaluation via 5-fold cross-validation.
use.validation: In the 5-fold cross-validation scheme, 10% of the training data in each fold was used for hyper-parameter tuning (validation role).
use.testing: In the 5-fold cross-validation scheme, 20% of the training data in each fold was used for testing the method. The official test set was not used due to lack of ground-truth labels.
use.other: Data augmentation (rotation, flipping, zoom, shift) was applied to these images to address class imbalance and prevent overfitting.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of the classification models on the ISIC 2018 dataset, which lacked ground-truth labels for its official test and validation sets.
To calculate accuracy, sensitivity, precision, and F1-score metrics to account for class imbalance in the datasets.
To prevent overfitting during the training process by stopping network training if classification accuracy on the validation set did not change for 5 epochs.
To divide the ISIC 2018 training data into five folds, ensuring each image is included in the test set at least once and preventing bias during the training process.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference between optimized pre-trained models and non-optimized pre-trained models (specifically, the two algorithms had the same error rate, or H0: θ12 > θ21 as stated in the text for the one-sided test formulation).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference (specifically, H1: θ21 ≥ θ12 as stated in the text).
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The null hypothesis was rejected for all class labels and overall performance (p-values were 0.006, 0.007, 2.76×10−12, and 2.90×10−9 respectively), indicating that the ResNet-50 model with optimized hyper-parameters performed significantly better than the model with default hyper-parameters.

results_obtained_in_each_dataset.extraction_error: Could not parse a balanced JSON object

architecture_used.main_model: Multiple architectures evaluated: DenseNet-201, ResNet-50, Inception-v3, VGG16, Xception (pre-trained models), and custom CNNs trained from scratch.
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained models were initialized with weights from the ImageNet dataset. Custom networks were trained from scratch.
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported (images were scaled down for optimization evaluation; specific input dimensions not stated).

custom_cnn_from_scratch.best_topology_example: 3 convolution layers, 2 pooling layers, 1 fully connected layer. First conv layer: 64 kernels size 7; second: 64 kernels size 2; third: 256 kernels size 5.
custom_cnn_from_scratch.topology_range: Network depth between 3 and 5 layers. Number of dense layers between 1 and 2. Dense layer neurons often 1024 or more.

training_details.best_optimizer_preference: Adadelta was preferred as the best optimization function across models in both datasets.
training_details.learning_rate_range: Optimized values reported (e.g., 0.0823 for ResNet-50/GA, 0.0551 for DenseNet-201/GA, 1e-05 for DenseNet-201/PSO).
training_details.batch_size: Optimized values reported (e.g., 32 for most models, 128 for Xception/GA).
training_details.separating_layer: Optimized values reported (e.g., layer 40 for ResNet-50/GA, layer 45 for DenseNet-201/GA).
training_details.early_stopping: Enabled; stopped if validation accuracy did not change for 5 epochs.

classification_or_output_head.feature_extraction: Deep features extracted from the last dense layer of the optimized models.
classification_or_output_head.fusion: Features from pairs of models were fused together.
classification_or_output_head.classifier: K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) classifier used on fused features. K value optimized between 2 and 70. Other classifiers tested (Decision Tree, MLP, SVM) but KNN performed best.

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation (rotation between 0 and 90 degrees, horizontal and vertical flip, random zoom, horizontal and vertical shift) applied to address class imbalance and prevent overfitting.
The study explicitly states that the proposed method performs classification without requiring heavy preprocessing or segmentation steps.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The above techniques were applied to each image in the ISIC 2018 and ISIC 2017 datasets, adding six additional images per original image to address class imbalance and prevent overfitting.

conclusions: The proposed method, which uses metaheuristic algorithms (Genetic Algorithm, PSO, DE) to optimize hyper-parameters and architecture of deep neural networks, outperforms similar methods in classifying skin lesions.
The method achieved an accuracy of 81.6% and an F1-score of 80.9% on the ISIC 2017 dataset.
The method achieved an accuracy of 90.1% and an F1-score of 89.8% on the ISIC 2018 dataset.
The proposed approach performs classification without requiring heavy preprocessing or segmentation steps.

Feature fusion of optimized models (specifically DenseNet-201 and Inception-v3 for ISIC 2018, and DenseNet-201 and ResNet-50 for ISIC 2017) improved classification performance compared to using single models.
The Adadelta optimization function was identified as the most effective optimizer across the tested pre-trained models (DenseNet-201, ResNet-50, Inception-v3, VGG16, Xception) for both datasets.

The classification performance for Actinic Keratosis (AKIES), Dermatofibroma (DF), and Melanoma (Mel) classes was lower than other classes, potentially due to low image counts or inter-class similarity.
The proposed method achieved results comparable to the winner of the ISIC 2018 challenge for the three-class classification task without using external data sources.
important_remarks: The study relies on the ISIC 2018 training set for evaluation because the official test and validation sets lacked ground-truth labels at the time of the study, using 5-fold cross-validation instead of a held-out test set.
Performance on specific classes (Actinic Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, and Melanoma) was lower than others, potentially due to low image counts or inter-class similarity.
The method avoids heavy preprocessing and segmentation steps, which is a noted strength for clinical deployment simplicity.
Class imbalance in the datasets was addressed via data augmentation (rotation, flipping, zoom, shift) and weighted average metrics rather than just accuracy.
The paper does not report external validation on independent datasets, limiting the generalizability of the findings beyond the ISIC 2017 and 2018 datasets.
